what_it_does: Static HTML/CSS desktop mockup of the opened toolbar presence dial (ui://toolbar/presence), showing eight right-hand-man posture modes from listening to off with focus selected, annotated to present the mode as a node (rhm_mode) on the system graph and to stress registry-fed directive text.
observations: It is a static design probe, not a functional runtime component.
The header comment explicitly maps the mockup to code references: suite.py MODE_DIRECTIVES/set_mode, App.tsx MODE_DESC/mode-sel, addresses.json ui://toolbar/presence, and governance.py.
The dial lists eight modes in a loud-to-quiet continuum: listening, text-only, background, focus, walkthrough, watch-and-react, decide-for-me, off.
The currently selected mode is focus, styled with an accent border and active volume bars.
A registry-fed note states that directive text should come from capabilities().mode_directives rather than a hand-copied front-end map.
The decide-for-me mode includes a governance strip explaining AUTO acts, SURFACE/CONFIRM asks, LOCKED never, and the system cannot self-approve.
data-ui-ref attributes attach landmark references such as ui://toolbar/presence and several subregions.
The left rail includes rhm_mode as a content node in the node palette.
A mobile tabbar placeholder is included with no matching CSS in this file.
standouts: Heavy grounding commentary that ties every visual claim to specific code files and line ranges.
Reframing of a mode dropdown as a graph node (rhm_mode) whose value governs the whole surface.
Governance posture strip for decide-for-me that distinguishes deterministic routing from AI confidence.
Landmark addressing via data-ui-ref, including the opened state of ui://toolbar/presence.
conventions: Follows the project design-system.css variable naming and component classes.
Uses the B4 / P4 panel-area-journey naming convention from the design system.
Employs data-ui-ref attributes to express UI landmark addresses.
Embeds explanatory design rationale in HTML comments, consistent with a staging probe.
Inline CSS is acceptable for a self-contained mockup but would be atypical for a production component.
concerns: The mode descriptions and glyphs are hand-copied into the mockup despite the stated goal of being registry-fed.
Several data-ui-ref sub-landmarks (e.g., /dial, /resp, /deep-work, /watch-and-react, /decide-for-me, /self-approve) are not obviously defined in the cited addresses.json from this file alone.
The mobile tabbar markup references classes with no corresponding CSS in this file, so its responsive behavior is unresolved here.
The decide-for-me governance strip uses SURFACE/CONFIRM as a combined label, which may conflate two distinct governance classes.
notes: A heavily annotated desktop mockup that tries to make the RHM presence mode obvious as a posture dial backed by a system graph node and registry directives.
questions: Are the data-ui-ref sub-landmarks (ui://toolbar/presence/dial, /resp, /deep-work, /watch-and-react, /decide-for-me, /self-approve) canonical entries in addresses.json or only illustrative?
Is the mobile tabbar styled and hidden on desktop in design-system.css, or is it unresolved in this mockup?
Will this dial replace the existing App.tsx <select className="mode-sel">, or remain a parallel design artifact?
Does the visual volume-bar mapping accurately reflect the relative loudness encoded in suite.py MODE_DIRECTIVES?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is explicitly labeled a staging design probe for the opened presence dial, and it delivers a static visual mockup with the stated grounding annotations.
apparent_themes: Presence as a node/posture, not a hidden preference.
Source-of-truth registry feeding the UI rather than duplicated front-end maps.
Governance posture, not model confidence, determines autonomy.
Recognize-by-sight continuum from loud/active to quiet/silent.
Design artifacts grounded in code references to prevent fictional behavior.
intention_signals: To persuade design and engineering to replace the existing mode dropdown with a visible, posture-first presence dial while insisting that the mode list and directive prose remain owned by the runtime registry and that high-autonomy modes are constrained by deterministic governance rules.
novelty: It treats a UI control as a node on the system graph and uses a visual volume/autonomy continuum plus a governance posture strip to make a deterministic decide-for-me mode legible.
contribution: A concrete, annotated visual layout for the opened toolbar presence control, including landmark addressing, the loud-to-quiet posture continuum, a registry-fed directive note, and a governance posture strip for decide-for-me, which a fused system could retain as the design reference for the RHM mode surface.
summary_for_embedding: Static HTML/CSS desktop mockup of the opened right-hand-man presence dial showing eight registry-grounded posture modes with focus selected, annotated as a system-graph node and emphasizing deterministic governance routing.
intention_for_embedding: To visualize how the existing mode select should be reconceived as a posture dial that makes RHM loudness and autonomy obvious while remaining tied to the runtime registry.
suggested_fields: registry_fed_status to record whether displayed values are actually sourced from capabilities().mode_directives or hand-copied
landmark_scope to distinguish canonical addresses.json entries from illustrative sub-landmarks
proposed_edge_kinds: grounds_in — a design claim is grounded in a specific code reference
represents_open_state_of — the dial is the opened state of a toolbar control
visualizes_node — a UI element visualizes a graph node such as rhm_mode
registry_fed_by — displayed text is intended to be fed by a runtime capability
